text: I think it's fair to say that finding
the positives in Starfleet Academy is a
bit like opening a daycare center in a
strip club. Yeah, I guess it could be
done in theory, but man, there's a whole
lot of obstacles in the way. After all,
we're witnessing the final death throws
of a 60-year-old franchise that survived
a decade of cancellation, the end of the
Cold War, and the visual and
intellectual diarrhea of the J.J. Abrams
movies. It's like watching your favorite
longretired boxer lumbering back into
the ring for one more fight. punch drunk
and overweight and a shadow of his
former self getting completely pummeled
and humiliated by an opponent half his
age. But leave it to that bastion of
hard-hitting journalism, the Mary Sue,
to cheer everyone up. Wait, do they
still exist? Wow. Ah, whatever. Let us
examine this fair and completely
unbiased article, shall we? Those dude
bros mad at Starfleet Academy aren't
actual Star Trek fans.
>> You sure about that?
You sure about that?
>> You see, dear viewer, Starfleet Academy
isn't actually an insult to Star Trek
storytelling cinematography character
development, or even basic human
dignity. And that's why the internet's
been dogging on it since its release in
much the same way that Disney Star Wars
is just as good as anything George Lucas
ever managed to produce.
Oh, no. It's actually a small minority
of toxic dude bros like myself and Gary
who just can't accept the greatness of
this show. Time and time again, we're
confronted with fans of something who
continue to miss the lessons within the
media they're consuming. We've seen it
with Star Wars, our superhero stories,
and it's become an increasing problem
with Star Trek fans. Oh dear, it's such
a drag being media literate, isn't it?
More recently, men like The Critical
Drinker and Nerd Rotic, known for hating
anything that seems to be left-leaning,
have been dogging on Paramount Plus's
slate of Star Trek shows. The issue with
these right-wing figureheads talking
about Tre is that they miss one
important detail about Star Trek. It was
never for them. Oh, really? Then pray
tell who exactly was it for? Because
I've spent most of my life watching and
enjoying Star Trek progressive messaging
and all. I grew up with it, in fact. And
I've spent dozens, if not hundreds of
hours online talking about my love for
the movies, the Next Generation, the
original series. [ __ ] man. I'm one of
those crazy people who even enjoyed
Voyager, the first Star Trek show led by
a female captain, no less. But since men
like these ex personalities think
they're the authority on all things
nerdy, the reality is they just speak
loudly and incorrectly about these
franchises. Look, you can use your money
to buy every Star Trek toy out there.
doesn't mean you understand what this
series is saying to you. And now with
the new series Star Trek Starfleet
Academy, we've seen an uptick in their
lack of understanding of what Gene
Rodenbury sought to do with Star Trek.
Really?
>> Well, it seems like we're not the only
ones who lack understanding. Mary Sue
writer, see, we don't claim to be the
final authority on anything. We're just
people with opinions. We put those
opinions out there on the internet and
we invite others to engage with them
however they see fits. If those opinions
are well thought out, capably argued and
backed up with knowledge and experience,
then well, people tend to listen to
them. The problem for you seems to be
that more people agree with our opinions
on Starfleet Academy than yours. Sucks
to be you, I guess. But that being the
case, maybe it's time you asked
yourselves why. In the great marketplace
of ideas that is the internet, why are
people rushing to consume ours while
yours gather dust in a bargain bin? Is
it perhaps because you're not being
entirely honest about this show's merits
and you're trying to dismiss any and all
criticism of it with the tired old
argument of this was never made for you?
The reality is that Star Trek was made
for everyone, no matter their background
or political persuasion. It was the very
definition of inclusive because it
presented its audience with ideas that
they were invited to consider instead of
propaganda that they were commanded to
accept. Whether it was questions of
ethics and morality, the value of the
individual versus the needs of the
collective, whether we have the right to
interfere in other societies even if we
believe we can do good, the nature of
justice, power and responsibility, even
what it means to be human. All of these
ideas and countless more could be found
in Star Trek. And what made the
franchise so accessible and timeless is
that they were often presented with
balance and care to show different
perspectives to show both sides of an
issue and trusting the audience to form
their own opinions instead of just
pushing one particular point of view to
the exclusion of all else. It was never
about moral judgment or sneering
lectures, but instead intellectual
curiosity, something we're shockingly
lacking these days. Star Trek until
fairly recently was a show of ideas. It
was a franchise created by serious and
mature people exploring serious and
mature concepts, usually led by a cast
of serious and mature characters. In
short, it was aspirational. It was about
the best of humanity pitting themselves
against the wild, surreal, and often
terrifying challenges the universe had
to offer. And you know what? It was
[ __ ] awesome. Starfleet Academy, on
the other hand, is none of those things.
At best, it's a trit and melodramatic
teen soap opera with all the angst and
relationship gripes and over wrought
emotions that come with it. Cartoonishly
over-the-top villains and a cast of
characters that I wouldn't trust to run
a Starbucks, never mind command a
starship and explore the galaxy.
Dialogue that's the writing equivalent
of verbal diarrhea written by aging
millennials acting like Gen Z trying so
desperately hard to be funny and edgy
and cool that it's like watching your
granddad trying to hang out in a Call of
Duty lobby. Star Trek for the Tik Tok
generation, for an audience of low
attention spans, low IQ, and low
interest. It's a show about idiots, made
by idiots for idiots. It's not
aspirational in any way, shape, or form
because it doesn't represent the best of
what humanity could be in the future. It
represents the worst of what it is right
now. Shallow, vapid, loud, meaningless,
and disposable, obsessed with identity
and feelings. A show about nothing that
possesses no ideas and provides no
perspectives. Unlike the good people of
the Mary Sue, I don't profess to be the
authority on all things Star Trek, but
in my humble opinion, I think Gene
Rodenbury would be disgusted to see
what's become of his creation. And quite
frankly, he's not the only one. All of
this is to say that Starfleet Academy is
what I've always loved about Star Trek.
It feels like a warm hug and is fun,
adventurous, and exactly the kind of
world that Rodenbury dreamt of 60 years
ago. So, don't let a bunch of nonsense
on social media try to paint the show in
any other way. Well, Mary Sue, I don't
know what to tell you. I've explained my
reasons why I think this show isn't
worth watching, but if you genuinely
think it's great and it makes you happy,
then well, it shouldn't really matter
what a bunch of kmoginly YouTubers have
to say about it, should it? Unless, of
course, there's a horrible little doubt
in the back of your mind that maybe
Starfleet Academy isn't the creative
triumph you've convinced yourself it is.
that maybe the reason so many people are
criticizing it is that well it actually
is just a bit [ __ ] I'll let you
decides.
Anyway, that's all I've got for today.
Go away now.
